title: Data-driven mapping of borophene growth pathways
authors: Colin Bousige, Jean Furstoss, Julien Lam, Pierre Mignon
abstract: Deterministic synthesis of borophene remains challenging because many polymorphs compete during nucleation and growth. Here we combine a reactive machine-learned interatomic potential with grand-canonical Monte Carlo simulations and data-driven structural classification to track borophene formation from early nuclei to extended layers on Ag(111) and Ag(100). We build temperature-pressure substrate growth maps and resolve how vacancy motifs, phase intermixing and seed structure govern polymorph selection. The simulations reproduce key experimental trends, including the prevalence of $β_{12}$/$χ_3$ phases and their temperature-dependent competition, while revealing kinetic pathways that connect metastable nuclei to long-range order. We identify conditions that suppress competing motifs and promote targeted phases, providing actionable synthesis windows. These results establish a predictive framework for directing borophene growth and, more broadly, for controlling polymorphism in low-dimensional materials by coupling atomistic simulation with machine-learning-enabled phase recognition.
summary: This work develops a predictive framework to control the synthesis of borophene polymorphs on silver surfaces. By combining machine-learned potentials with Monte Carlo simulations, the authors demonstrate that growth is governed by kinetic pathways, not just thermodynamic stability. This allows them to identify specific temperature and substrate conditions that favor desired, stable phases.
main_problem: The deterministic synthesis of borophene is challenging due to the competition between numerous polymorphs during nucleation and growth.
main_result: The study establishes a predictive framework by showing that polymorph selection is governed not just by thermodynamic stability, but by kinetic pathways that allow for self-propagation.
method: The authors combined reactive machine-learned interatomic potentials with grand-canonical Monte Carlo simulations and employed machine learning classifiers trained on local atomic descriptors.
model_or_system: The system is borophene, a 2D boron sheet, grown on Ag(111) and Ag(100) substrates. The simulation tracks the evolution from early nuclei to extended layers.
key_observables: Growth maps (temperature-pressure substrate maps), the proportion of different allotropes over time, and the self-propagation metric ($N_{\infty}^{\square}/N_0^{\square}$).
important_parameters: Temperature (e.g., 500 K vs. 700 K) and substrate type ($	ext{Ag}(111)$ vs. $	ext{Ag}(100)$) are key controlling parameters.
main_assumptions: The classification algorithm relies on a probability threshold (0.4) for identifying allotropes, and the simulation assumes the MLIP accurately captures the necessary physics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate snapshots of the 21 allotropes, t-SNE projections of descriptors, melting temperature comparisons, and time evolution of vacancy counts during growth.
paper_structure: The paper follows a structure of defining the problem (polymorphism), developing a multi-stage simulation pipeline (MLIP + GCMC + ML classification), presenting results showing kinetic control over stability, and concluding with actionable synthesis windows.
why_interesting: The rigorous coupling of advanced classical simulation techniques (GCMC, MLIP) with data-driven classification methods provides a powerful template for predicting phase behavior in complex, low-dimensional materials, which is relevant for understanding quantum confinement effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Snapshots of the 21 different allotropes included in the training dataset in this study. The nomenclature (α, β, χ, and δ) refers to the coordination of the boron atoms, while the subscript has no particular meaning and is only used to distinguish between different allotropes (except for the δ allotropes, where the subscript indicates the coordination). We selected these allotropes because (1) they include all those experimentally observed on silver27 (α, β8, β10, β12, and χ3) as well as those synthesized on copper, gold, and aluminum (α1, α5, χ3), and (2) they cover the full range of vacancy densities ν (from 0 for δ6 to 1/3 for δ3), a critical parameter for borophene properties....

FIG. 2. Data-driven classification of borophene al- lotropes. a. Dimensionality reduction of the LMBTR de- scriptors for the different allotropes in the training set through a two-components t-SNE projection. Each point corresponds to a vacancy in the structures obtained during the growth process, and the color corresponds to the allotrope it belongs to. Even with a 2-components t-SNE projection, the different allotropes are quite well separated in the descriptor space. We use a 155-components PCA projection for the actual analysis, which allows for an even better separation. b. Snapshot of the final structure from Fig. 5a at 700 K on Ag(111) with the different allotropes identified by the...

The N 0 □and N ∞ □quantities are the number of va- cancies attributed to the β12 allotrope at the beginning and at the end of the simulation, respectively. b. Relative vari- ation of the number of vacancies attributed to the allotrope that was used in the seed during the growth process as a function of the temperature and orientation. The variation is taken as the last value of the simulation from Figs. S7-S8 divided by the first one, N ∞ □/N 0 □. Statistics were acquired only for P = 10−3 Pa, which is the...

FIG. 5. Analysis of a full-scale growth. a. Snapshots of the structures obtained at regular stages of the growth process on Ag(111) and Ag(100). The box size is ∼50 × 50 Å2, and some identifiables allotropes are highlighted in white. b. Number of vacancies of each allotrope along the full-scale GCMC growth process. The unknown vacancies are not shown for clarity.
